value.market_read: WTI Curve: WTI settled at $67.84 in the prior session, firming after the latest WPSR (week ending June 26) showed a prompt-supportive crude draw that tightened near-term balances. The reported draw pulled barrels out of Cushing and lifted the front month, reinforcing an already-backwardated structure. The M1–M12 spread stands at $1.19 in backwardation — a shallow but clear signal that the market is pricing modest prompt tightness rather than a storage surplus, with the front cluster ($67.84 Aug) sitting above deferred 2027 tenors near $66.55. The M1–M2 spread is a slim 5 cents, indicating the prompt tightness is present but not acute. Watch: Monitor whether Cushing continues to draw, which would steepen front-end backwardation and widen the M1–M2 spread. Brent Curve: Brent settled at $70.94, holding above WTI as the global benchmark reflected steadier waterborne balances. Notably, the Brent curve is not cleanly backwardated at the very front — the second and third months ($71.43, $71.86) trade above the prompt, a mild prompt-month dip that flattens into a broadly humped shape. The M1–M12 spread is essentially flat at just $0.10, implying the market sees global crude balances as roughly neutral over the coming year rather than acutely tight. This contrasts with WTI's firmer backwardation, underscoring that the recent tightening impulse is more a domestic US/Cushing story than a global one. Watch: Watch the front-month Brent structure for whether the prompt catches up to M2–M3 as summer demand and OPEC+ supply decisions unfold. RBOB Gasoline Curve: RBOB settled at $2.932, supported by peak summer driving-season demand pulling on prompt barrels. The curve is steeply backwardated through the balance of summer, falling from $2.932 (Aug) to roughly $2.20 by early 2027 before the seasonal winter-to-summer-2027 kink lifts it back toward $2.42. The M1–M12 spread of $0.5595 reflects strong prompt pull as summer-grade specifications and driving demand tighten near-term supply, with the seasonal roll-down into fall/winter grades explaining most of the steepness. This is a classic demand-driven backwardation, not a supply-scarcity signal. Watch: Monitor the pace of the seasonal roll-down heading into the late-summer transition to winter-grade gasoline, which typically deflates the front. ULSD Diesel Curve: ULSD settled at $3.2136, the firmest of the products complex on tight distillate balances. The curve is smoothly and steeply backwardated, declining from $3.2136 (Aug) to $2.6839 by mid-2027 with no seasonal kink — a monotonic slope that signals genuine prompt tightness in distillate rather than a purely seasonal effect. The M1–M12 spread of $0.5297 mirrors the gasoline steepness but its uniform shape is more consistent with structurally lean inventories. Tight diesel keeps the distillate crack supported and underpins refinery run economics into summer. Watch: Watch distillate inventories and any early pull toward heating-season restocking, which would keep the backwardation steep.
value.kpi_wti: WTI settled at $67.84, firming on a prompt-supportive crude draw in the latest WPSR that tightened near-term Cushing balances.
value.kpi_brent: Brent settled at $70.94, with a nearly flat M1–M12 spread signaling globally neutral crude balances despite the firmer US prompt.
value.kpi_rbob: RBOB settled at $2.932, held up by peak summer driving demand pulling on prompt gasoline barrels.
value.kpi_ulsd: ULSD settled at $3.2136, the firmest of the complex on structurally tight distillate inventories.
value.kpi_bw: The Brent–WTI spread narrowed to $3.10 as WTI firmed relative to Brent, weakening the US export arb and encouraging barrels to stay domestic.
value.kpi_curve_regime: Crude sits in shallow backwardation (WTI M1–M12 at $1.19, Brent near-flat at $0.10) while products show steep, demand-led backwardation, implying prompt tightness concentrated in refined fuels rather than a broad crude-scarcity regime.
